74685.subject: This may surprise you: SpaceX is buying Cursor for $60 billion
74685.body:
The Atlas

Автор: GenAI Works


Автор: GenAI Works

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/may-surprise-you-spacex-buying-cursor-60-billion-genai-works-spuue?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BxAKtxS2hQrG5RxF72pmh0Q%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=0Yq8yyKjld7sk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mqicuezy~z5-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mqicuezy-z5
  
Welcome back! Pause for a second. SpaceX's reported $60 billion Cursor deal shows that owning the developer workspace may now matter more than building another model. A new CVPR paper shows that better image segmentation was already hidden inside frozen vision models. The trick was using the features differently. Databricks launched Omnigent to help different AI agents work together instead of running separately. And Microsoft made Copilot Cowork generally available with usage-based pricing, turning agent work into something companies can track, consume, and meter like cloud computing. In today's Atlas Newsletter: Why SpaceX paid $60 billion for Cursor: the AI war stopped being about the biggest model and became a land grab for where work happens. Segmentation with zero training and one example: a CVPR paper pulls a hidden bias out of frozen features and the accuracy was waiting there all along. Your agents do not talk to each other: Databricks open-sourced Omnigent, a meta-harness that sits above Claude Code, Codex and Pi to compose, control and share them. Microsoft just made agent work a metered utility: Copilot Cowork hit general availability with usage-based pricing, Microsoft's first major pricing shift in nearly two decades. PS: Want to collaborate with the biggest GTM engine and distribution moat for AI companies? You can feature your tool with us and reach millions. Why did SpaceX buy Cursor for $60 billion? Ever notice this pattern? The biggest AI moves lately are not about models. They are about real estate. SpaceX just agreed to buy Anysphere, the company behind Cursor, in an all-stock deal worth about $60 billion.  The merger was signed June 16, days after SpaceX's blockbuster IPO.  A rocket and satellite company buying a coding assistant sounds strange until you see what they actually bought. They did not buy a model. xAI already has Grok. They bought the place developers sit all day. Cursor's revenue tells the story. It hit a $4 billion annualized run rate, more than double where it sat in February.  That growth is not coming from autocomplete. It is coming from owning the surface where engineers decide what to build, what to refactor, what to test and what to ship. Here is the pattern once you start looking for it. Anthropic, OpenAI and Google have spent two years fighting over the same layer. Claude Code, Codex and Cursor kee

🧠 AI's Crypto News Brief: Markets turned cautious as Kevin Warsh's hawkish Fed debut held rates steady and pushed Bitcoin toward $64.5K. Yet beneath the macro chill, institutional plumbing kept building: tokenized stocks, Solana credit ratings, and fresh Bitcoin-backed DeFi rails. 🏦 Warsh's Hawkish Fed Debut Holds Rates, Bitcoin Slips The Federal Reserve left its benchmark rate unchanged in Kevin Warsh's first meeting as chair, but a more hawkish-than-expected committee dimmed hopes for near-term cuts. Bitcoin slid toward a week-to-date low near $64.5K as risk appetite cooled into the decision. Read full story 🏛️ SEC Moves to Greenlight Tokenized Stock Trading The SEC is reportedly preparing an innovation exemption that would let platforms trade tokenized equities on-chain, a potential bridge between traditional securities and blockchain rails. Backers frame it as a market shakeup; critics warn of gaps in investor protection. More details 📊 Moody's Brings Credit Ratings to Solana Ratings giant Moody's is embedding credit scores directly into blockchain-based securities through its Token Integration Engine, now live on Solana mainnet. The aim is to make tokenized real-world assets more legible to institutional investors. Read more 🚫 UK Sanctions HTX Over $1.5B in Russia-Linked Flows Britain designated crypto exchange HTX under Russia-related sanctions rules, with TRM Labs tying it to roughly $1.5 billion in flows connected to sanctioned entities. It marks another escalation in enforcement against exchanges accused of moving illicit funds. See details ⚖️ HyperFund Promoter Pleads Guilty in $1.89B Fraud Florida promoter Rodney Burton pleaded guilty over his role in the $1.89 billion HyperFund scheme, one of the largest crypto fraud cases to reach the courts. The plea signals intensifying legal exposure for influencers who market fraudulent yield programs. Read full story ₿ Circle Launches cirBTC, a 1:1 Bitcoin-Backed DeFi Asset USDC issuer Circle rolled out cirBTC on Ethereum, a fully backed wrapped-Bitcoin product aimed at institutional DeFi collateral. The launch pushes Circle deeper into the contested market for tokenized Bitcoin, well beyond its stablecoin franchise. More details 💵 Bipartisan Deal Would Block a Fed CBDC Until 2030 A bipartisan agreement in Congress would bar the Federal Reserve from issuing a ce

74681.subject: $150M Shorts. Gone.
74681.body:
----------
_**Your Weekly Deep Dive into The World of Crypto**_

**June 17, 2026 · ****5 Min Read**

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/3e6fcf76-0903-4907-8dee-7fd009d13f9d/image.png?t=1781678321)
Caption: **Market Snapshot  · As of June 17**_**  |  **_**BTC Price **_** |  **_**YTD Return **_** |  **_**Fear & Greed **_** |  **_**ETF Flows (Weekly) **_** **_

## 📡** THIS WEEK IN CRYPTO**

**GM, Readers!**

Last week, crypto faceplanted. This week, crypto faceplanted into a trampoline.

Bitcoin clawed back $6,700 per coin from its $59,130 weekend low, the ETF outflow streak finally snapped, roughly $150 million in shorts got incinerated by Monday morning, and Trump posted a peace deal into existence at 5:30 PM ET on Sunday. By Monday brunch, BTC punched through $65,000 for the first time in twelve days.

_**Let’s get into it...**_


----------View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/6c53930f-b4d7-4e7d-baee-6a88bb56a6da/image.png?t=1781097813)
Caption: 

----------
# 🐋 THE BIG WHALE MOVES

### **1. The Short Squeeze Heard Around the World**

Sunday at 5:30 PM ET, Trump[ posted on Truth Social](https://truthsocial.com/@realDonaldTrump/posts/116750587569914985) that the US-Iran war was over and the signing is scheduled for Friday, June 19, in Switzerland. By Monday open, roughly $150 million in crypto shorts had been forcibly closed, IBIT had taken in $57.7 million on Friday alone, and BTC tagged $65,880, a 12-day high.

The short side had been comfortable. After three weeks of relentless ETF outflows and a $59K weekend test, the consensus trade was sideways chop into the FOMC. Then it got run over by a Truth Social post.

_**Markets stopped rewarding the patient bear and started rewarding the inconvenienced bull.**_

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/071e9854-c426-4934-8401-a4294f87aab1/image.png?t=1781678380)
Caption: 

### **2. The H Token Hack, or Was It?**

On June 9, attackers drained roughly $32 million from 17 wallets tied to Humanity Protocol’s H token foundation on Ethereum, then bridged the exploit to BNB Chain and minted 100 million H tokens worth about $12.9 million. The token crashed from roughly $0.70 to $0.08 in one session, an 87% wipe.

Then it got weird. ZachXBT publicly questioned whether the incident was staged, citing wallet patterns that did not match a typical key compromise. The H team denied involvement. Investigation ongoing.

_**Either the attackers got lucky, or the team got cute. Holders ate it.**_

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/0675d69e-7f33-4efa-8b5a-e4cd6380156b/image.png?t=1781678408)
Caption: 

### **3. Saylor Hits the Bid Again**

Strategy is buying again

74679.subject: America’s bull market has entered its manic phase
74679.body:
<link rel=stylesheet href=https://marber-cdn.economist.com/foundations/latest/css/font-face.css>
 The Economist

 
 


 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
https://click.e.economist.com/?qs=ABB7InYiOjEsImQiOjQ5MTB9AAcAAAAAAtROavYpO1gtUACc1pyFazmtMj4NwMsrnhVEEXae_kD1bxDlo_wvD5iT0YaFkR_eUM7ScbmBWzxJ7zFUWcmJ-904MV0tSIZIy9HnZRD6O3g 

June 17th 2026

 

 
 
 
The Economist Today

 Our best journalism, handpicked each day


 
 
 

 
https://click.e.economist.com/?qs=ABB7InYiOjEsImQiOjQ5MTB9AAcAAAAAAtROavYqEgXDftWre0IenXJ2ds16tvyoYW1ceAPePxtS1JYyR5UqzL9idDJTZ40NKfZ4gRfJPRfOQjz8d3REeO-iRoLJdRktfo6ZB0rRmYM 

https://click.e.economist.com/?qs=ABB7InYiOjEsImQiOjQ5MTB9AAcAAAAAAtROavYrRzJ3UEWq_6JjmieJnFPX1jmiSFX5qnFoa4K8eyUNVc3kdLeX5pSRCfZGhycWBbYTmO81GKlAvFqOXwt-1PNAZR0WqVDUrxaat_w 



 
 

Brooke Unger

Senior digital editor



 How can you tell when a stock-market bubble is in fact a bubble, and therefore prone to burst? It’s when mania takes hold. And how on earth do you spot that? The options market provides the clues. 
 
https://click.e.economist.com/?qs=ABB7InYiOjEsImQiOjQ5MTB9AAcAAAAAAtROavYsT2G9IOE92FspQwfsT3N_VQMKQYHiD3N2DUR4mv12gCCtBlTrjjrcd8N47hcNuG3qbJNkestN7duAb_FJ1atNxMfiTmzwWQ 
Our Buttonwood columnist 
 interprets them.


 From one sort of extreme to another: this year’s El Nino climatic pattern could be the strongest on record. 
 
https://click.e.economist.com/?qs=ABB7InYiOjEsImQiOjQ5MTB9AAcAAAAAAtROavYtw4Ao8kli6mOQibmclkatqIGBHR26fJZtzvaAqhwv905v3kwYLVGcWIvCuGk5zJ-qzI8OEPFRpYxrePAQLr9hTA4bS7Yw6Q 
We analyse 
 how that will change the
